questions.scenario: During a rollout, the `orders-api` pods are replaced. The frontend should not track individual pod IPs. Which Kubernetes object gives a stable internal address for the changing pods?
questions.explanation: A Kubernetes service gives a stable network identity for pods that can be replaced during scaling or rollout.

questions.scenario: A public request reaches an AKS-hosted API through the standard HTTP edge path. Which layers might be involved before application code handles the request?

options.text: An external load balancer or edge entry.
An ingress controller and ingress rule.
A Kubernetes service selecting ready pods.
A VM image gallery choosing the app version on every request.

questions.explanation: AKS request paths often include cloud edge, ingress, service selection, pod readiness, and application logs. VM image choice is not per-request routing.

questions.scenario: The deployment says three replicas are desired, but none of the pods become ready. Which statement is most accurate?

options.text: The service should still send production traffic because the desired replica count is three.
The control plane accepted desired state, but workload health still depends on pods becoming ready.
Ingress will repair the container image automatically.
The node pool is irrelevant because pods never run on nodes.

questions.explanation: Kubernetes can store desired state even when the actual pods fail. Read deployment rollout, pod readiness, events, and logs.
